meta.title: The Sports Page — Concept Registry
meta.purpose: Maps each statistical concept across the 3-tier curriculum ladder: an ISSUE demonstrates it (see one), a PRIMER teaches it (play / do one), a GradStats-Book CHAPTER gives the full treatment (learn / teach one). A new concept is a new entry; empty 'issues' or null 'primer' marks a coverage gap to fill.

book.title: Statistics by Us for You (GradStats-Book)
book.branding: arms-length companion textbook — neutral front door, no author-name emphasis in newsletter-facing copy
book.note: Front door LIVE (GitHub Pages, HTTP 200, 'Statistics by Us for You') as of 2026-07-16. Note: URL path is case-sensitive (GradStats-Book, not gradstats-book). Book is being built out in a separate session — deep-link to chapters only once their rendered paths are stable; root front door is safe.

concepts.title: Measurement: Building a Metric
concepts.summary: A metric is built, not found: a number stands in for a trait you can't touch. Then the only two questions that matter — reliability (does it agree with itself?) and validity (does it measure what you claim?). Anchor issue: the 'acceleration' metric and its window problem.

concepts.title: All Models Are Wrong (and Overfitting)
concepts.summary: Box: all models are wrong, some useful. A line through every data point forecasts nothing (overfit); a useful model is honest about its error and ignores the outliers we fixate on. Anchor candidates: pre-season simulations, prediction-framework issues, 'the prior isn't a promise.'

concepts.title: Do We Believe the Data, the Model, or the Theory?
concepts.summary: Data-fitting vs model-fitting vs the theory a model instantiates. When the three disagree, which do we trust? The epistemic keystone — hardest to make sport-punchy, so it comes after 14 and 15 prove the template.

concepts.title: Why Research Design Matters
concepts.summary: The study you run decides what you are allowed to conclude — you cannot rescue a bad design with fancy statistics. What you measured, on whom, compared to what. The deepest frontier concept and arguably the most important.

concepts.title: Communicating Uncertainty
concepts.summary: How to say what you do not know without either false precision or useless hedging — ranges, intervals, 'best guess plus honest error.' Pairs with precision-vs-accuracy (No. 7) and the Sunday-Edition prediction scoring.

concepts.title: Heuristics: Biases vs. Ecological Rationality
concepts.summary: Kahneman & Tversky: heuristics as systematic biases. Gerd Gigerenzer: fast-and-frugal heuristics as often optimal in the real world. The productive tension — when is a shortcut a bug, and when is it the smart move? Sports intuition is the proving ground.

concepts.title: Information & Surprise
concepts.summary: A result you expected teaches nothing; one you couldn't call teaches a lot. Surprise = log2(1/p); its average is entropy, maximized at a coin-flip (1 bit). It grades forecasts via log loss. Anchor issue: which 2026 CFB weeks matter — toss-ups between contenders carry the most bits; blowouts carry almost none. Pairs with communicating-uncertainty (No. 18) and p-value (No. 11).

concepts.title: Rates of Change: Level, Velocity, Acceleration
concepts.summary: Three questions inside 'how good is that team': level (the value), velocity (slope / 1st derivative — which way, how fast), acceleration (2nd derivative — is the trend strengthening or reversing). A rolling average lags by design; acceleration leads. The tax: every derivative amplifies noise, so you smooth, which reintroduces lag — a bias-variance dial. Anchor issues: CFB program trajectories (#109, Indiana's average lags a title / Nebraska's turn) and the acceleration metric (#097). Pairs with regression-to-the-mean (No. 3).

concepts.title: The Unit of Analysis
concepts.summary: What entity does a number actually describe? Measuring a team/offense/ensemble and pinning it on a person is a unit-of-analysis error (a pitcher's 'win', a QB's stat line, a coach's record). Data are nested (plays<players<teams<leagues); reading an individual off group output is the ecological fallacy, and teammates' non-independence makes a person-level ranking look sturdier than it is. Recover the individual only with a design that varies them across casts (trades/portal = natural experiment; adjusted plus-minus). Anchor issue: the QB-tiers critique (#110). Siblings: research-design (No. 17), measurement (No. 14).

concepts.title: Variance
concepts.summary: A number can only tell cases apart to the extent it varies; a constant predicts nothing. Var(sum)=Σ Var of the parts (independent), so the highest-variance components dominate an additive outcome, and a predictor's pull scales with coefficient×its own variance. Guardrail: variance is NECESSARY but not SUFFICIENT — it must also correlate with the outcome (fantasy is the clean case: categories ARE the score). Anchor issue: 'variance is king' fantasy (#112, a WR's good week = 10x a bad one; the matchup is decided in the high-swing slots). Part 2 waiting: favorites suppress variance, underdogs chase it. Siblings: communicating-uncertainty (No.18), rates-of-change (No.21).

concepts.title: The Objective Function: What Are You Actually Maximizing?

Recently added: information & surprise (20), rates of change (21), unit of analysis (22), variance (23). Planned Part-2 issue: variance strategy (favorites suppress, underdogs chase).
